# RFP: Refactoring ŌĆö Prototype

*Generated by `/propose-refactoring` on 2026-04-06.*

A code quality scan of `proto/` (all crates, excluding `proto/target/`) identified the
following smells. This RFP requests a design proposal addressing the highest-priority items.
No code changes are made here ŌĆö this document is input to the `/propose` skill.

---

## Scope

All `.rs` files under `proto/`, excluding `proto/target/` and test-only modules
(`#[cfg(test)]`). Scripts were run against the full scope; manual review focused on
the top offenders by line count and complexity score.

---

## Detection Summary

| Script | Findings |
|--------|----------|
| long-functions | 44 functions exceeding 50 lines |
| complexity-check | 10 HIGH files (Ōēź50 branch points), 16 WARN files (20ŌĆō49) |
| detect-duplicates | Several structurally repeated patterns; most are test boilerplate |
| unwrap-check | 102 in `ast_builder.rs` (production), 57 in `interpreter.rs` (mostly tests), 7 `expect()` in shell entry points |

---

## Findings

### High Priority

#### H1 ŌĆö Yielding/Unyielding Code Duplication in interpreter.rs

**Location:** `proto/ish-vm/src/interpreter.rs:231` and `proto/ish-vm/src/interpreter.rs:2103`
**Smell:** God function / implicit coupling
**Description:** `exec_statement_yielding` (667 lines) and `exec_statement_unyielding`
(498 lines) are near-identical `match` dispatchers on `Statement`. Likewise,
`eval_expression_yielding` (437 lines) and `eval_expression_unyielding` (277 lines) mirror
each other for `Expression`. Together these four functions account for ~1,880 lines, most of
which is duplicated logic. Any bug in one variant must be manually mirrored to the other.
The `Statement::Assignment` arms handling `AssignTarget::Property` and `AssignTarget::Index`
are word-for-word identical across both `exec_statement_*` variants (lines 247ŌĆō297 vs.
2117ŌĆō2164). `Expression::Literal` evaluation is also identical in both `eval_expression_*`
variants.

The yielding/unyielding split is intentional (async Rust cannot conditionally await), but
pure-sync sub-computations inside these functions could be extracted as shared helpers called
by both variants, substantially reducing the duplication.

**Suggested refactoring:** Extract shared, sync-safe logic into private `fn` helpers:
- `fn eval_literal(lit: &Literal) -> Value` ŌĆö called by both `eval_expression_*` variants
- `fn apply_assign_target(target: &AssignTarget, object_val: Value, index_val: Value, assign_val: Value) -> Result<(), RuntimeError>` ŌĆö called by both `exec_statement_*` variants
- `fn eval_binary_op_values(op, lhs, rhs)` is already extracted; continue the same pattern for other shared arms

This does not require collapsing the yielding/unyielding split ŌĆö it only extracts the arms
whose bodies are identical between the two variants.

---

#### H2 ŌĆö 102 Panicking `unwrap()` Calls in ast_builder.rs

**Location:** `proto/ish-parser/src/ast_builder.rs` (102 `.unwrap()` calls in production code)
**Smell:** Panic-on-error
**Description:** The AST builder uses `.next().unwrap()` pervasively on pest `Pairs`
iterators, e.g. `inner.next().unwrap()` at lines 100, 127, 128, 138, 190, 240, etc.
These are "grammar-guaranteed" ŌĆö if the grammar rule matched, the token must be present ŌĆö
but they panic with an unhelpful message if the grammar and builder ever diverge. At 102
call sites, a single grammar refactor can trigger a cascade of opaque panics rather than
structured `ParseError` values. Numeric parsing also uses `.parse().unwrap()` (lines 1564,
1568) where out-of-range inputs would panic rather than produce a user-visible error.

**Suggested refactoring:**
1. Introduce `fn expect_next<'i>(pairs: &mut Pairs<'i, Rule>, ctx: &str) -> Result<Pair<'i, Rule>, ParseError>` that returns a structured `ParseError` instead of panicking.
2. Replace all `inner.next().unwrap()` with `expect_next(&mut inner, "<rule name>")?`.
3. Replace `s.parse::<i64>().unwrap()` with `s.parse::<i64>().map_err(|e| ParseError::from_str(e, span))`.

This converts 102 panic sites into proper `Result` propagation without changing any
externally observable behaviour.

---

### Medium Priority

#### M1 ŌĆö Boilerplate-Heavy `register_ast_builtins` in reflection.rs

**Location:** `proto/ish-vm/src/reflection.rs:769` (380 lines)
**Smell:** God function / repeated match arms
**Description:** `register_ast_builtins` registers ~20 AST constructor builtins by repeating
the same four-step pattern for each: call `env.define`, call `new_compiled_function` with
empty param vecs, check arity, build a `HashMap` with string keys, return `Value::Object`.
The structure is mechanically identical across every entry; only the function name, expected
arity, and field names differ. This makes adding a new AST node type unnecessarily verbose
and error-prone (copy-paste the block, forget to update the arity check, etc.).

**Suggested refactoring:** Introduce a local helper or macro:
```rust
fn ast_node_builtin(name: &str, fields: &[&str]) -> Value { ... }
```
that accepts a name and an ordered field list, builds the arity check and `HashMap` assembly
automatically. Reduces 380 lines to approximately 50.

---

#### M2 ŌĆö Arity-Check Boilerplate Repeated Across builtins.rs

**Location:** `proto/ish-vm/src/builtins.rs:199`, `:377`, `:511`, `:663`, `:106`
**Smell:** Repeated match arms / implicit coupling
**Description:** Every builtin function body begins with an identical arity check:
```rust
if args.len() != N {
    return Err(RuntimeError::system_error("fn_name expects N argument(s)", ErrorCode::ArgumentCountMismatch));
}
```
This pattern appears 30+ times across `register_strings`, `register_lists`,
`register_objects`, `register_conversion`, and `register_io`. Additionally, `new_compiled_function`
is always called with `vec![], vec![], None` for the param/type/return-type arguments in
every builtin definition, since builtins don't participate in the typed parameter system.

**Suggested refactoring:**
1. Extract `fn arity(name: &str, args: &[Value], n: usize) -> Result<(), RuntimeError>` called at the top of each builtin closure.
2. Add a `fn new_builtin(name: &str, f: impl Fn(&[Value]) -> Result<Value, RuntimeError> + 'static) -> Value` convenience wrapper that hard-codes the empty param/type vecs, simplifying every `env.define(...)` call site.

---

#### M3 ŌĆö Duplicated Brace-Scanning Logic in `interpolate_shell_quoted`

**Location:** `proto/ish-vm/src/interpreter.rs:3200` (63 lines)
**Smell:** Repeated match arms
**Description:** The function handles three variable-substitution syntaxes ŌĆö `$var`,
`${var}`, and bare `{var}` ŌĆö but the brace-scanning loop for `${var}` (lines 3209ŌĆō3218) is
almost a copy of the brace-scanning loop for `{var}` (lines 3240ŌĆō3255). Both scan forward
for `}`, extract the name, and call `resolve_shell_var`. The only difference is the start
offset and the leading character (`$` vs. nothing). The `.unwrap()` call on
`name.chars().next().unwrap()` (line 3247) inside the `{var}` branch is guarded by
`!name.is_empty()` but is still a panic site if the guard is ever removed.

**Suggested refactoring:** Extract `fn scan_to_close_brace(chars: &[char], start: usize) -> Option<(String, usize)>` and call it from both branches. Eliminate the inline `.unwrap()` by using `name.starts_with(|c: char| c.is_ascii_alphabetic() || c == '_')`.

---

### Low Priority

#### L1 ŌĆö Missing Exit-Code Sentinel Constant in interpreter.rs

**Location:** `proto/ish-vm/src/interpreter.rs:1794` and `:1803`
**Smell:** Primitive obsession
**Description:** The value `-1` is used as the sentinel for "process exited without a code"
in two adjacent call sites:
```rust
let exit_code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1) as i64;
```
This is a minor readability issue ŌĆö the meaning of `-1` is not self-documenting at the call
site.

**Suggested refactoring:** Define `const MISSING_EXIT_CODE: i64 = -1;` near the top of the
interpreter module and use it in both places.

---

#### L2 ŌĆö Large Flat Display Impls in display.rs

**Location:** `proto/ish-ast/src/display.rs:29` (233 lines) and `:265` (107 lines)
**Smell:** Large enum arms (structural size, not logic complexity)
**Description:** Two `fmt` implementations for AST node types total 340 lines. Each `match`
arm is simple (1ŌĆō5 lines of `write!`), making the file hard to navigate but not incorrect.
This is a structural-size issue only.

**Suggested refactoring:** Low urgency. If the file grows further, split by node category
into `display_stmt.rs` and `display_expr.rs`. No change needed now.

---

## Refactoring Patterns Reference

| Pattern | When to apply |
|---------|--------------|
| Extract function | Function body exceeds ~50 lines or has distinct phases |
| Consolidate match arms | Two or more `match` blocks on the same enum with identical arms |
| Replace unwrap with `?` | `.unwrap()` / `.expect()` in `fn` returning `Result` |
| Extract module | Group of tightly related items that currently live in a larger file |
| Introduce type alias | Repeated complex type signatures |
| Newtype wrapper | Primitive obsession ŌĆö bare `String` or `usize` used for distinct concepts |
| Introduce helper/macro | Structurally identical boilerplate blocks differing only in names/arities |
